text:
RESTAGING BONE SCINTIGRAPHY AND CT ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH CONTRAST

PATIENT: Raymond Kowalski
CLINICAL INDICATION: 69-year-old male with known stage IV metastatic castration-sensitive prostate cancer (Gleason 9, Grade Group 5) with osseous and nodal metastases. Currently on androgen deprivation therapy with leuprolide acetate and abiraterone acetate/prednisone initiated approximately 18 months prior. PSA at last check 1.1 ng/mL (down from 187 ng/mL at diagnosis). Restaging examination for treatment response assessment.

REFERRING PHYSICIAN: Medical Oncology

EXAMINATIONS PERFORMED:
1. Whole-body planar and SPECT/CT bone scintigraphy (Tc-99m methylene diphosphonate)
2. CT abdomen and pelvis with intravenous contrast (portal venous phase)

COMPARISON: Prior staging bone scan and CT abdomen/pelvis performed approximately 18 months ago at the time of initial diagnosis.

---

BONE SCINTIGRAPHY FINDINGS:

Technique: Approximately 25 mCi of Tc-99m MDP was administered intravenously. Whole-body planar images were acquired in anterior and posterior projections at approximately 3 hours post-injection. Supplemental SPECT/CT images were obtained through the axial skeleton, pelvis, and proximal femora.

Axial Skeleton:
The previously documented foci of intensely increased radiotracer uptake in the L3 and L4 vertebral bodies are substantially diminished in intensity compared to the prior study. The lesions now demonstrate only mildly heterogeneous uptake that has transitioned toward a pattern more consistent with sclerotic healing rather than active osteoblastic metastatic disease. The morphology and density characteristics on the low-dose CT component of the SPECT/CT are notable for increased sclerotic density at these sites, a finding in keeping with a favorable osseous treatment response. There is no new abnormal uptake identified in the thoracic or cervical spine. No new axial skeletal lesions are identified.

Pelvis:
The previously identified focus of increased uptake in the right iliac wing has markedly diminished in intensity and extent. On the SPECT/CT correlate, the corresponding area in the right iliac wing demonstrates increased bony density/sclerosis without aggressive periosteal change or cortical destruction, consistent with a treated/healing metastatic lesion. No new pelvic osseous lesions are identified. The sacrum, pubic symphysis, and ischial tuberosities demonstrate symmetric, physiologically appropriate background uptake without focal abnormality.

Proximal Femora:
The previously described focus of mildly increased activity in the right femoral neck has improved substantially. On SPECT/CT, this area now demonstrates only faint residual uptake with associated sclerotic change, without evidence of cortical breakthrough or pathologic fracture risk. Left proximal femur is unremarkable. No new femoral or lower extremity osseous lesions detected.

Thoracic Cage and Sternum:
The ribs, sternum, and clavicles demonstrate symmetric physiologic radiotracer distribution without focal areas of increased uptake to suggest metastatic involvement. No new lesions are identified.

Skull and Appendicular Skeleton:
No abnormal radiotracer uptake is identified in the calvarium, facial bones, upper extremities, or distal lower extremities. Degenerative uptake is noted in the bilateral acromioclavicular joints and lumbar facet joints, consistent with the patient's age and known history of occupational exposure as a steelworker.

Background Soft Tissue:
Renal activity is symmetric. Bladder is well-visualized. No unexpected soft tissue foci of increased uptake.

---

CT ABDOMEN AND PELVIS WITH CONTRAST FINDINGS:

Technique: Axial images obtained from the lung bases through the pubic symphysis following the administration of intravenous iohexol contrast. Multiplanar reconstructions performed. Oral contrast was administered.

Liver and Biliary System:
The liver is normal in size and contour. No focal hepatic lesions identified. No biliary ductal dilatation. The gallbladder is unremarkable without evidence of cholelithiasis or wall thickening.

Spleen:
The spleen measures approximately 10.2 cm in craniocaudal dimension. No splenic lesions. No splenomegaly.

Pancreas:
The pancreas is normal in morphology and attenuation throughout its length, including head, neck, body, and tail. No pancreatic ductal dilatation or peripancreatic stranding. No pancreatic masses identified. Note is made of the patient's family history of pancreatic adenocarcinoma (brother); however, no pancreatic abnormality is evident on this examination.

Adrenal Glands:
Bilateral adrenal glands appear normal in size and configuration. No adrenal nodules or masses. Note is made that the patient is on abiraterone with prednisone; adrenal morphology is unremarkable.

Kidneys and Ureters:
Bilateral kidneys are normal in size and contour with symmetric contrast enhancement and excretion. No renal masses, hydronephrosis, or calculi identified. No urothelial thickening.

Bladder and Prostate:
The urinary bladder demonstrates a small volume of urine and has no focal wall thickening or intraluminal mass. The prostate gland remains enlarged, measuring approximately 42 cc (previously approximately 45 cc at diagnosis, representing minimal interval change likely attributable to ADT effect). The prostate gland has heterogeneous internal architecture without a discrete dominant mass identifiable on CT; however, CT has limited soft tissue resolution for intraprostatic characterization. No peri-vesicular fat stranding to suggest local inflammatory change.

Lymph Nodes:
The previously enlarged right obturator lymph node has decreased in short-axis diameter, now measuring approximately 0.7 cm in short axis (previously 1.2 cm at the time of initial staging approximately 18 months ago). The node retains a somewhat rounded configuration but is below the threshold for size-based suspicion, and the interval reduction is substantial and consistent with a treatment response. No other enlarged pelvic sidewall, external iliac, internal iliac, or common iliac lymph nodes are identified. No enlarged retroperitoneal lymph nodes along the aorta, inferior vena cava, or at the aortic bifurcation. No abnormal mesenteric lymphadenopathy.

Ascites and Peritoneum:
No free intra-abdominal fluid. No peritoneal nodularity or omental caking.

Bowel:
The visualized small and large bowel loops are within normal limits. No obstruction, wall thickening, or mass effect. No intussusception or mesenteric abnormality.

Vascular Structures:
The abdominal aorta demonstrates mild atherosclerotic calcification without aneurysmal dilatation. The inferior vena cava is patent. No iliac vessel aneurysms. No evidence of venous thrombosis in the imaged pelvic vasculature.

Osseous Structures (CT correlate):
As noted on the SPECT/CT images, the previously identified sites of metastatic involvement at L3, L4 vertebral bodies, right iliac wing, and right femoral neck demonstrate interval development of increased sclerotic density. No new aggressive osseous lesions or cortical destruction are identified. No pathologic fractures. No spinal canal compromise identified at the visualized vertebral levels.

Lung Bases:
The bilateral lung bases visualized on this examination appear clear, without consolidation, pleural effusion, or nodularity in the imaged portions.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. FAVORABLE TREATMENT RESPONSE: The previously identified osseous metastatic foci at L3, L4, the right iliac wing, and right femoral neck have all substantially improved in radiotracer avidity and now demonstrate sclerotic changes on CT correlate, consistent with healing/treatment response following approximately 18 months of ADT and abiraterone/prednisone. No new osseous lesions are identified anywhere in the visualized skeleton.

2. NODAL RESPONSE: The right obturator lymph node has decreased from 1.2 cm to 0.7 cm in short-axis dimension, consistent with a meaningful nodal treatment response. No other pathologically enlarged lymph nodes are identified in the retroperitoneum or pelvis.

3. NO VISCERAL METASTASES: No hepatic, splenic, adrenal, or renal metastatic lesions identified. No peritoneal disease.

4. Overall imaging findings are compatible with a partial response to systemic therapy, with significant improvement in the burden of osseous and nodal disease compared to the prior staging study.

5. Mildly atherosclerotic aorta without aneurysm. Stable prostate gland size with slight interval reduction likely reflecting ADT effect.

Reporting Radiologist: [Nuclear Medicine / Diagnostic Radiology]
Electronically signed
